bibliographic.citation: George Henslow, Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century, Together with a List of Plants Recorded in Contemporary Writings, with Their Identifications, with an explanatory introduction and notes by Walter W. Skeat (London: Chapman and Hall, Limited, 1899).
bibliographic.title: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century, Together with a List of Plants Recorded in Contemporary Writings, with Their Identifications

entities.description: George Henslow's printed compilation and transcription of Middle English material from four manuscripts, with an explanatory introduction and notes by Walter W. Skeat and a separate plant list not included in CME00012.
entities.label: Henslow, Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century (1899)
notes: CME00012 encodes the title page and printed pp. 1-73 and 76-145 only.
The complete volume's preface, Skeat's explanatory introduction, pp. 74-75, plant list on pp. 147-278, and index are outside the cataloged unit.
James Britten reviewed the excluded plant list; he is not assigned responsibility for the cataloged selection.

rights.basis: U-M presents this edition-derived selection as public-domain material under No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: The 1899 printed edition material selected in CME00012
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This record does not make a worldwide determination for every editorial or image component.

entities.description: A vellum medical and technical commonplace book owned by George Henslow in 1899. Henslow printed the English material found discontinuously between manuscript pp. 26 and 258. Its present location and complete modern catalog identity were not established.
entities.label: Henslow manuscript A
notes: Henslow says one of his sons found the manuscript in the library of the late J. Johnstone and gave it to him.
No public evidence found in this review justifies describing the witness as lost or destroyed.

physicalDescription.format: Quarto commonplace book in contemporary wooden boards covered with leather
notes: Henslow reports that most of the book is in one hand and that some initials are illuminated.
The description is historical and has not been checked against the physical manuscript.

next_steps: Trace George Henslow's estate, library, donation, and sale records and the earlier J. Johnstone provenance.
Contact relevant manuscript repositories and the Middle English Dictionary source-bibliography maintainers with the incipit, physical description, and frontispiece.
Once located, request the complete physical object rather than only the CME-selected pages.
notes: Unknown present location is not evidence that the witness is lost.
openQuestions.question: Where is the physical manuscript that Henslow designated manuscript A, and what is its current catalog identity and provenance after 1899?

next_steps: Locate manuscript A before pursuing its complete imaging.

Request complete-codex photography of Harley MS 2378, Sloane MS 2584, and Sloane MS 521 through British Library Digitisation Services, including exterior surfaces, endleaves, blanks, and adjacent works.
Obtain written reuse permission and record the resulting provider terms before caching any supplied files.
notes: The cached one-image manuscript-A bundle is explicitly partial, and the commercial Harley image is neither complete nor openly licensed.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable digital facsimiles of all four physical manuscript objects be obtained?

next_steps: Align Henslow's legacy page milestones against complete manuscript images or repository foliation concordances.
Record each discontinuous selected passage rather than reducing the selection to a continuous span.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact modern folio locators for every passage selected by Henslow from Harley MS 2378 and Sloane MS 2584?

next_steps: Ask U-M Library for item-level keying specifications, source-copy identification, and selection rationale.
Compare any production documentation with the structural review notes in TEMPHEAD.
openQuestions.question: What item-specific production record explains U-M's selection of the title page and pp. 1-73 and 76-145 while omitting pp. 74-75 and the rest of the volume?

reviewNotes: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus edited selection, not the complete 1899 volume and not a single medieval work or codex.

Printed pp. 74-75 are absent from the XML even though they lie between manuscript sections A and B; source coverage is therefore recorded as title page, pp. 1-73, and pp. 76-145 rather than pp. 1-145.
Witness descriptions, printed editorial layers, digital encoding, repository artifact, and image surrogates are modeled as distinct entities.
Dates attributed by Henslow and Skeat to the selected material are retained as historical scholarly claims and not substituted for current manuscript-catalog dating.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact copies University of Michigan CME item CME00012, an edition-level selection from George Henslow's 1899 Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century. The encoded selection comprises the title page and printed pp. 1-73 and 76-145: four discontiguous manuscript-derived sections from Henslow's privately held manuscript A, British Library Harley MS 2378, Sloane MS 2584, and Sloane MS 521. It omits the printed preface and introduction, pp. 74-75, the plant list on pp. 147-278, and the index. A complete Royal College of Physicians copy of the 1899 volume is cached. Only one printed photographic specimen of manuscript A and one commercially licensed, unidentified page of Harley MS 2378 were found publicly; no complete public facsimile was found for any of the four physical witnesses, and the current location of manuscript A remains unknown.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval compilers
catalogSummary.date: Medieval source material, later fourteenth to fifteenth centuries, with unresolved dating for manuscript A; edition published 1899
catalogSummary.editor: George Henslow, with commentary by Walter W. Skeat
catalogSummary.region: England; more specific composition places are uncertain and witness-specific dialect claims are not generalized to the whole selection
catalogSummary.title: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century

catalogingSubject.label: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection consisting of the 1899 title page and Henslow's four manuscript-derived sections on printed pp. 1-73 and 76-145. It is not the complete 1899 volume, which also contains a preface, Skeat's explanatory introduction, material on pp. 74-75, a plant list on pp. 147-278, and an index; nor is it any one medieval manuscript or a single organically unified medieval work.

contentParts.label: Section A: Henslow manuscript A
notes: The four verse lines present in this DIV1 are an editorial quotation nested in a note and do not change the section's cataloged prose form.
contentParts.summary: The English portions of Henslow's private commonplace book, beginning with cloth-dyeing instructions and continuing with medical recipes, charms, and related practical material.

contentParts.label: Section B: Harley MS 2378
contentParts.summary: Selected Middle English medical, culinary, confectionery, technical, and prognostic material from the composite Harley manuscript, with items repeated from section A generally omitted.

contentParts.label: Section C: Sloane MS 2584
contentParts.summary: Selected Middle English medical and technical recipes from Sloane MS 2584, including the 33-line verse 'A preface of this booke.'

contentParts.label: Section D: Sloane MS 521
contentParts.summary: Selected Middle English remedies, charms, and prognostic material from the multilingual composite Sloane manuscript.

assertion.notes: This summarizes surviving source layers and historical editorial dating; it is not a single composition date for the heterogeneous selection.

dateStatements.display: Later fourteenth to fifteenth centuries, with unresolved dating for manuscript A
notes: The title's phrase 'of the Fourteenth Century' is an editorial label and is not used as uniform dating evidence.

assertion.notes: Historical editorial estimate; not verified against the unidentified physical manuscript.

dateStatements.display: Before 1400 according to Henslow and Skeat

assertion.notes: Preserved alongside, not reconciled with, the before-1400 estimate.

dateStatements.display: ?a1500 in the Middle English Dictionary source citation

assertion.notes: This date must not be generalized to the whole composite witness or all of section B.

dateStatements.display: Circa 1395 for two section-B recipes printed on pp. 120-122

scope.description: Only the two recipes mapped by the BL component record to Harley MS 2378, ff. 157r-158r
First publication of Henslow's edited selection as represented here

extent.description: Four top-level manuscript sections: A, B, and D are prose; C is mixed prose and verse with one substantive 33-line verse group. The raw XML has two LG elements and 37 L elements, but one LG/four L form an editorial Chaucer quotation nested in a section-A note and are excluded from intrinsic-work metrics. The XML also contains 535 P elements, 144 PB elements, 531 MILESTONE elements, and 726 NOTE elements; paragraph and note counts are encoding structure, not a defensible count of individual recipes or works.

formStatements.description: Predominantly prose recipes and instructions, with a substantive 33-line verse prologue in section C.
Sections A, B, and D are cataloged as prose; the four verse lines encoded inside a section-A editorial note are not intrinsic section content.
Section C combines prose recipes with the 33-line verse 'A preface of this booke.'

assertion.notes: The manuscript-derived text is predominantly Middle English even though the XML's coarse TEXT/@LANG value is 'eng'.

scope.description: Title page, section headings, introductions within sections, glosses, and notes

assertion.notes: Latin occurs within formulas, headings, quotations, prayers, and manuscript-derived passages; it is not the dominant cataloged language.

notes: Work metadata describes the edition-level digital selection; witness-level holdings, facsimile availability, and reproduction rights remain in the linked lineage manifest.
The catalog title retains Henslow's historical wording while date assertions explicitly preserve later and conflicting evidence.
The full-title plant list is modeled as a related but excluded work component, not silently folded into CME00012.
No responsibility is assigned to James Britten because his documented role concerns the excluded plant list.

next_steps: Define an item-boundary policy that distinguishes headings, ingredients, procedures, embedded charms, and editorial notes.
Align each proposed item against complete manuscript images before replacing the current section-level inventory.
Record Henslow's omissions and summaries rather than treating each XML P element as an item.
notes: The current manifest intentionally leaves numbered_items null.
openQuestions.question: How many distinct medieval recipes, charms, prognostics, and other textual units are present after Henslow's selections, omissions, summaries, and duplicate suppression are modeled consistently?

next_steps: Consult the Index of Middle English Prose, Middle English Dictionary source bibliography, recipe and charm indexes, and modern manuscript scholarship.
Create item-level assertions only where exact passage alignment and evidence permit.
Keep witness copying dates distinct from composition dates.
openQuestions.question: Can individual selected texts be assigned distinct composition dates, earlier attestations, source traditions, authors, translators, or compilers?

next_steps: Trace George Henslow and J. Johnstone provenance records using the manuscript's physical description and frontispiece incipit.
Ask MED bibliography maintainers and likely repositories for the source behind the ?a1500 citation.
Replace the conflicting historical estimates only when the physical witness or a current authoritative description is identified.
openQuestions.question: What is manuscript A's current identity, holding institution, date, provenance, and dialect localization?

next_steps: Obtain complete manuscript images or repository foliation concordances.
Align each Henslow legacy page milestone and editorial omission against modern foliation.
Update part locators from broad spans to exact discontinuous lists once verified.
openQuestions.question: What exact modern folios correspond to every discontinuous passage selected from Harley MS 2378 and Sloane MS 2584?

assertion.notes: The broad English context is supported; no single locality is assigned to all four source groups.
No named medieval author or compiler is established for the cataloged selection as a whole.

scope.description: The four medieval manuscript-derived sections collectively, without implying a single compiler

assertion.notes: Henslow assembled the four manuscript sections and suppressed repeats in later sections.

scope.description: The manuscript-derived text in sections A-D
Notes embedded in the four selected sections; the separately printed explanatory introduction is outside CME00012

assertion.notes: This describes plant ingredients and remedies within the selected sections, not the excluded systematic plant list.

subjects.label: Confectionery, culinary preparation, and food preservation

summaries.value: An edition-level selection of Middle English medical, technical, culinary, ritual, and prognostic material transcribed by George Henslow from four manuscript miscellanies and published in 1899 with notes by Walter W. Skeat. The selection is predominantly prose but includes a 33-line verse prologue in section C.

Section A prints the English portions of Henslow's private manuscript A; sections B-D give selected passages from British Library Harley MS 2378, Sloane MS 2584, and Sloane MS 521. Henslow generally suppresses recipes in B-D that duplicate section A.

CME00012 includes the 1899 title page and printed pp. 1-73 and 76-145. It excludes the preface, Skeat's explanatory introduction, pp. 74-75, the plant list on pp. 147-278, the index, and other book matter. The encoded title page therefore names content that the cataloged digital selection does not contain.

The heterogeneous four-part selection is cataloged at edition level. Its medieval textual units are not counted individually because XML paragraphs and Henslow's editorial divisions do not provide a reproducible one-to-one item inventory.

assertion.notes: This is the short title displayed for the edition-level selection; it does not claim that all selected source material is securely fourteenth-century.

titles.value: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century

assertion.notes: The long title describes the complete 1899 volume; the plant list named in it is outside the CME00012 cataloging boundary.
